title_en: Trump Declared a Space Race With China. The US Is Losing
title_zh: 特朗普宣布与中国展开太空竞赛。美国正在输球

paragraphs.en: The senator wanted a promise. A solemn vow. For the last six years—or maybe the last decade or quarter century, depending on how you count it—the United States and China had been locked in a space race, a contest to see which nation could put its people on the moon. Senator Ted Cruz wanted President Donald Trump’s nominee to run NASA, Jared Isaacman, to pledge that the US would not lose.
paragraphs.zh: 参议员想要的是一个承诺。一个庄严的誓言。过去六年里——或者按你的口径算，过去十年，甚至是过去四分之一个世纪——美国和中国一直处在太空竞赛中，争夺哪一个国家先把自己的人民送上月球。参议员泰德·克鲁兹希望唐纳德·特朗普提名担任NASA局长的人、贾里德·艾萨克曼，作出保证：美国不会输掉这场比赛。

paragraphs.en: Cruz brought a little surprise to Isaacman’s confirmation hearing last April. It was a poster of the moon. On one side stood three astronauts and a giant Chinese flag. On the other were two more figures in space suits, with the tiniest Stars and Stripes planted in the lunar soil. Cruz apologized for the imbalance. “My team used ChatGPT,” explained the senator, who chairs the committee that oversees NASA.
paragraphs.zh: 去年四月，克鲁兹在艾萨克曼的确认听证会上带来了一个小惊喜——一张月球海报。海报一边站着三名宇航员和一面巨大的中国国旗；另一边则是两个穿着太空服的身影，月球土壤里只插着一面极小的星条旗。克鲁兹为这幅海报的不对称道了歉。“我的团队用了ChatGPT，”这位主持NASA监督委员会听证会的参议员解释说。

paragraphs.en: Then Cruz, with a bit more seriousness, asked Isaacman, “Do we have your commitment that you will not allow the scenario on the right of this poster to happen? That China will not beat us to the moon?”
paragraphs.zh: 随后，克鲁兹更严肃地问艾萨克曼：“你能否郑重承诺，不允许这张海报右侧出现的情形发生？也就是，不让中国比我们先到月球吗？”

paragraphs.en: Isaacman, a billionaire entrepreneur who had paid for his own missions to space, replied, “Senator, I only see the left-hand portion of that poster.”
paragraphs.zh: 艾萨克曼是一位亿万富翁企业家，他曾自费完成自己的太空任务。他回答道：“参议员，我只看得见那张海报左边的部分。”

paragraphs.en: It was a red-meat, fuck-yeah, pitch-perfect response. And Isaacman may have meant it. But by the time of his testimony, the Trump administration had started a process that would lay waste to NASA, pushing nearly 4,000 agency employees to quit. Then the White House proposed a massive, 24 percent cut to NASA’s budget. Then Trump yanked Isaacman’s nomination and named a new part-time acting chief, a fellow who boasted in his official NASA biography that he is one-half of “America’s first and longest-married reality TV couple.” Then that guy picked a fight with Elon Musk, who’s building NASA’s moon lander. And Isaacman was back in the running. In December, Trump capped off the year with an executive order pushing Americans to get back to the moon by 2028.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一番激情四射、让人高喊“没错”的、堪称点题的回应，Isaacman可能也确实是这么想的。但在他作证时，特朗普政府已经启动了一场几乎要毁掉NASA的流程，导致该机构近4,000名员工辞职。随后，白宫提出了对NASA预算的惊人24%削减。接着，特朗普撤回了Isaacman的提名，改任命了一位新的兼职“代理局长”，这位新任者在其官方NASA简介里还夸耀自己是“美国第一对且婚姻持续时间最长的真人秀夫妻之一”的一半。然后，这个人又与正在为NASA建造月球着陆器的埃隆·马斯克发生了争执。于是Isaacman又重新进入竞争。到了12月，特朗普以一项行政命令为一年画上句号，要求美国在2028年前重返月球。

paragraphs.en: If all of this sounds suboptimal to you, welcome to the club, space ranger. That dysfunction is one of many reasons why the vast majority of the two dozen sources I interviewed for this story believe that China will put people on the moon first. I spoke with nine former NASA officials who served at the highest rungs of the space agency under presidents Trump and Biden; none of them were optimistic about America’s chances. “We did the worst of all worlds,” one of the nine tells me. “We positioned it as a race without planning to win.”
paragraphs.zh: 如果你觉得这一切都不够理想，欢迎加入“太空游侠”俱乐部。这种混乱是我采访本篇文章中近两打消息源时，他们大多认为中国将率先把人送上月球的诸多原因之一。我采访了九位前NASA高官，他们在特朗普和拜登任内都在该机构担任过最高层职位；没有一位对美国的胜算持乐观态度。一位受访的九人之一告诉我：“我们做的是最糟糕的方案组合。”“我们把它包装成一场竞赛，却没有赢的打算。”

paragraphs.en: Here are 23 ways China is rewiring the future.
paragraphs.zh: 以下是中国正在重塑未来的23种方式。

paragraphs.en: The original space program was the ultimate symbol of America at its screaming-eagle apex. Rocket scientist was shorthand for brilliant, and many of them were working in Huntsville, Alabama, aka Rocket City. The word astronaut was synonymous with grit, and you could find the gutsiest of them in Houston. Moonshot was (and is) code for something borderline impossible. Space races have helped spur the development of everything from the integrated circuit to the solar panel to 5G. But that was before America decided to stab itself in the brain.
paragraphs.zh: 最初的太空计划曾是美国在其巅峰期最响亮的象征。‘火箭科学家’几乎是天才的代名词，当时许多人都在阿拉巴马州亨茨维尔——也就是“火箭城”——工作。‘宇航员’一词几乎等同于坚韧，而你最有胆识的人往往可以在休斯敦找到。所谓“登月计划”一直都意味着一件几近不可能完成的任务。太空竞赛推动了从集成电路到太阳能板再到5G的各类技术发展。但这一切都发生在美国决定朝自己脑袋开刀之前。

paragraphs.en: Today, much of the world drives Chinese electric cars, powers their homes with Chinese solar panels, and stays in touch with made-in-China phones. Chinese scientists have eclipsed their American counterparts in the production of high-quality research, and the White House has responded by gutting American science funding and charging $100,000 to let in highly skilled immigrants. So if Chinese astronauts step down from their lander and livestream the results in 4K—and to be clear, it’s still an “if” at this point—it’ll be more than a point of national pride for Beijing. It’ll be a declaration that the American Century is officially over.
paragraphs.zh: 今天，世界上许多人开着中国制造的电动汽车，用中国太阳能板为家庭供电，并通过中国制造的手机保持联系。中国科学家在高质量研究产出上已超越美国同行，而白宫对此的回应是大幅削减美国科学拨款，并向高技能移民征收10万美元的入境费用。也正因为如此，如果中国宇航员从着陆器走下来，并用4K直播探测结果——明确说，现在这仍是“如果”——这不仅会成为北京的一次民族自豪时刻。它更像是在宣告：美国世纪已经正式结束。

paragraphs.en: About 18 months ago and some 3,000 miles above the Earth, the Chinese robotic spacecraft Chang’e-6 released its reentry capsule, which skipped in and out of the atmosphere before parachuting into the grasslands of China’s Inner Mongolia. Inside were about 4 pounds of moon rocks and soil. It was the first sample return from the far side in human history.
paragraphs.zh: 大约18个月前，在地球上空约3000英里处，中国无人航天器“嫦娥六号”释放了其再入舱，再入舱在大气层中一会进入一会退出，最终降落在中国内蒙古的草原上。舱内装有约4磅的月球岩石和月壤样本。这是人类历史上第一次从月球背面带回样本。

paragraphs.en: Putting people on the moon might seem like a so-what sequel, coming 50-plus years after Apollo. But there’s a reason no one’s been back to the moon in half a century. It is maddeningly difficult to put anything down there, even little unmanned landers. A handful of soft-landing missions have succeeded, most of them Chinese. More numerous are the flops of the Japanese, Russian, Israeli, American, and Indian space programs.
paragraphs.zh: 让人们登陆月球听起来似乎只是阿波罗计划50多年后的无关紧要续篇，但半个多世纪以来没有人再返回月球是有原因的。真正让人抓狂的是，在那里降落任何东西都非常困难，即使是小型无人着陆器也不例外。少数几次软着陆任务取得成功，其中大多数是中国的；失败的任务则更多见于日本、俄罗斯、以色列、美国和印度的航天计划。

paragraphs.en: The reasons for failure are many—light gravity and uneven terrain make it easy for a lander to tip over; no atmosphere means firing rockets to brake; that firing kicks up clouds of dust that can limit visibility. “Once you start slowing down, within a matter of single-digit seconds, you are on an intercept course with the moon, and you don’t have enough propellant to go back to lunar orbit and try again,” says Will Coogan, a chief engineer at Firefly Aerospace, the only company to pull off a fully successful robotic moon landing, ever.
paragraphs.zh: 失败的原因有很多——低重力和不平整地形会让着陆器很容易倾覆；没有大气层意味着要靠火箭发动机减速；而这次减速会扬起尘土云，降低可见度。火鸟航天（Firefly Aerospace）首席工程师威尔·库根（Will Coogan）表示，该公司是唯一一次完成过完全成功的无人月球着陆尝试的公司。“一旦你开始减速，在区区几秒钟之内，你就会进入与月球的碰撞轨道，而且你没有足够的推进剂返回月球轨道并再试一次。”

paragraphs.en: Operations around the lunar poles are an “order of magnitude” tougher, a person who oversaw NASA’s lunar program tells me. Getting into the proper orbital plane requires more maneuvering. For consistent communication with ground control, you need to position a relay satellite around the moon. (As China did, about six weeks before landing Chang’e-6.) Once you’re there, be careful. Miles-deep craters are shadowed in near-eternal night, which means no solar power and temperatures that can drop below –200 degrees Celsius.
paragraphs.zh: 一位监督过美国宇航局月球计划的人告诉我，围绕月球两极的任务要困难一个数量级。要进入合适的轨道平面，需要更多机动。如果要与地面控制实现稳定通信，还必须在月球周围部署中继卫星（正如中国在嫦娥六号着陆前约六周所做的那样）。到达月球后，必须格外小心。月球两极附近有许多数英里深的陨坑长期处于近乎永恒的黑夜中，这意味着没有太阳能可用，温度还会降到零下200摄氏度以下。

paragraphs.en: Yet that’s exactly where China’s heading. Scientists believe the poles have enormous pockets of ice—and potentially helium-3, an isotope that’s extremely scarce on Earth. Helium-3 could unlock quantum computing and possibly nuclear fusion, many experts believe. “Let’s say on the lunar surface, helium-3 becomes a new source of fusion power,” as Isaacman told Cruz at that April confirmation hearing. “It could shift the balance of power here on Earth. I don’t think we can afford to find that out the hard way.”
paragraphs.zh: 但这正是中国的方向。科学家们认为两极地区拥有巨大的冰袋，并可能含有氦-3——一种在地球上极其稀有的同位素。许多专家认为，氦-3可以用于量子计算，甚至有望推动核聚变。“比如说，在月球表面，氦-3将成为一种新的核聚变能源来源，”艾萨克曼在四月的确认听证会上对克鲁兹说，“它可能改变地球上的力量对比。我认为我们不能等到以困难的方式才发现这一点。”

paragraphs.en: Both the US and China have dreamed of building a base at the south pole, and both would like to claim the prime landing sites. The upcoming Chang’e-7 mission aims to be the first to extract water from the moon. One desirable landing site is a spot high on the ridge of the Shackleton Crater, near the lunar south pole, at a location that’s almost always light. Chang’e-7 will carry instruments from Russia and several American allies—Italy, Switzerland, Thailand, Egypt, and Bahrain.
paragraphs.zh: 美国和中国都曾梦想在南极建立一个月球基地，而且双方都想先占领最佳着陆点。即将执行的嫦娥七号任务目标是成为首个从月球提取水的任务。一个理想的着陆区是在月球南极附近的夏克尔顿陨坑山脊上方的一处几乎始终有光照的地点。嫦娥七号将搭载来自俄罗斯及多国美国盟友——意大利、瑞士、泰国、埃及和巴林——提供的仪器。

paragraphs.en: The Chinese National Space Administration says the launch should happen in August. “When they say they’re going to do something by x, they tend to do it,” says Dean Cheng, a senior fellow at the Potomac Institute for Policy Studies who’s been tracking the progress.
paragraphs.zh: 中国国家航天局表示发射应在八月进行。“他们一旦说要在某个时间之前完成某事，通常都会做到，”追踪进展的华府政策研究协会高级研究员岑迪恩说。

paragraphs.en: China’s space efforts aren’t as sophisticated as America’s; Beijing is only now testing reusable rockets. And every space program faces mishaps. In November, three Chinese astronauts were stranded aboard their space station for nine days after a piece of orbiting junk damaged their return capsule. But China’s crewed lunar effort, for now, seems to be more or less on target. The idea is that a pair of rockets—one carrying the crew and service capsules, the other a lander—will head to the moon. They’ll rendezvous in lunar orbit, and a pair of astronauts will set foot on the surface. Exactly where they’ll land hasn’t been confirmed. But testing for all of the components is well under way. A spokesperson for the China Manned Space Agency has said the mission will launch “before 2030.”
paragraphs.zh: 中国的太空努力并不如美国那样成熟；北京现在才开始测试可重复使用的火箭。而且每个太空项目都会遇到事故。11月，三名中国航天员在一块绕轨垃圾损坏了他们的返回舱后，被困在空间站里长达九天。不过，就目前而言，中国的载人登月计划大体上还算按部就班。其设想是发射两枚火箭——一枚携带乘员及服务舱，另一枚携带着陆器——前往月球。它们将在月球轨道会合，随后两名航天员将登上月球表面。至于他们将在哪里着陆，目前尚未确认。不过，所有部件的测试都已在紧锣密鼓地进行。中国载人航天中心的一位发言人表示，该任务将在“2030年前”发射。

paragraphs.en: When will American astronauts get there? That’s a bit more complicated.
paragraphs.zh: 美国宇航员何时能够抵达？这件事则复杂得多。

paragraphs.en: Warning: the recent history of the US program to return astronauts to the moon is so contradictory, so politicized, so frustrating, it’s going to make your head hurt. As Jim Bridenstine, who served as Trump’s first NASA administrator, recently told Congress, “This is an architecture that no NASA administrator that I’m aware of would have selected.”
paragraphs.zh: 警告：美国把宇航员送回月球的计划最近历史，充满了矛盾、政治化，而且令人十分沮丧，以至于让人头疼。正如曾任特朗普第一任NASA局长的吉姆·布里登斯坦最近在国会所说，“这是一套体系结构，在我所知任何一任NASA局长看来都不会选。”

paragraphs.en: “No way to run a program, in my opinion,” says the NASA official who helped oversee the lunar initiative. “You can’t make this shit up. Really can’t. It’s really crazy.”
paragraphs.zh: “在我看来，没有办法这样运行一个项目。”“你不可能把这事编出来。真的不可能。这个太疯狂了。”

paragraphs.en: The original Apollo program was insanely ambitious, but there was a clarity to the mission. One giant rocket, carrying one tiny crew capsule and one even tinier landing module. The capsule would orbit the moon, and the lander would ferry astronauts to and from the surface. That was all. The new US moon program—Artemis, named after Apollo’s twin sister—isn’t nearly so neat.
paragraphs.zh: 最初的阿波罗计划野心可谓疯狂，但任务目标非常清晰。一个巨大的火箭、一个微小的载人飞船舱，以及一个更小的着陆舱，火箭只需将舱体送入轨道，登月舱再把宇航员往返于月面。这就是全部内容。新的美国登月计划阿耳忒弥斯——以阿波罗的“孪生姊妹”命名——就没有这么简洁。

paragraphs.en: For starters: The main rocket and capsule are made up of leftovers. Back in 2010, the Senate ordered NASA to build a rocket with old engines from the space shuttle program and repurpose a capsule from a Bush-era deep-space project. The rocket, known as the Space Launch System, would lift off carrying astronauts in a capsule named Orion. The combination of SLS and Orion has cost taxpayers tens of billions of dollars. It has flown exactly once. A second flight is slotted for February.
paragraphs.zh: 首先，主力火箭和飞船舱本身就是“接盘”工程。早在2010年，美国参议院就要求NASA采用航天飞机计划中的旧发动机，并改造一枚源自小布什时代深空项目的舱体。那枚被称为空间发射系统（SLS）的火箭，计划把宇航员送入太空，并把他们装在名为猎户座（Orion）的飞船舱里。SLS与猎户座的组合已让纳税人掏出数百亿美元。它目前只飞行过一次，第二次飞行已排在二月。

paragraphs.en: The Orion capsule was built on the heavy side, to give astronauts extra protection against radiation and other risks. But the capsule’s propulsion was under-powered to reach a tight lunar orbit. (The European Space Agency, which provided the thing, said it couldn’t afford to supply more.)
paragraphs.zh: 猎户座飞船舱做得偏重，是为了给宇航员提供更多辐射和其他风险防护。不过，这种设计导致它的推进力不足以进入紧凑的月球轨道。（提供该部分的欧洲航天局表示，他们负担不起再提供更强的配置。）

paragraphs.en: NASA engineers figured out, however, that the capsule could reach a farther-out, super-elliptical orbit, and that orbit would still allow for landings on the lunar poles. The drawback: At that orbit, completing a circuit of the moon takes nearly a week. Once you drop astronauts off, they’re stuck on the surface for days and days. A tear in a spacesuit or a malfunction in the lander’s life support system might turn into a nightmare.
paragraphs.zh: NASA工程师于是算出，飞船其实可以进入一个更远的、超椭圆轨道，而该轨道同样可以支持在月球两极降落的任务。问题在于：在那种轨道上，绕月一周几乎要花上近一周时间。宇航员一旦被送上月面，就会被困在那儿整整好几天。空间服一旦破损，或着陆器生命维持系统出现故障，都可能演变成一场灾难。

paragraphs.en: NASA also concluded that it needed to put a miniature space station in orbit around the moon. I must’ve heard a half-dozen rationales for this space station. It wouldn’t necessarily help with rescue efforts, but it could communicate with astronauts on the ground. And during later, longer-duration missions, it would give astronauts a more hospitable place to work and rest. Maybe.
paragraphs.zh: 美国航天局还认为自己需要在月球轨道上放置一个微型空间站。我当时大概听到过六七种左右的理由。它未必能直接帮助救援行动，但可以与着陆点附近的宇航员通信。而在之后更长时间、持续时间更久的任务中，它还能给宇航员提供一个更舒适的工作和休息场所。也许是那样。

paragraphs.en: Some at NASA view the so-called lunar “Gateway” as a kind of insurance policy. As one former official told me, “There’s the belief that if you have these pieces pre-positioned in space, we’ll have a long-term program that Congress is less willing to walk away from.” All of this was meant to take a decade or so to come together—like, 2028 or 2029. Until Mike Pence stepped up to the mic.
paragraphs.zh: 一些NASA内部人士把所谓的“月球门户”视为一种保险机制。正如一位前官员告诉我的：‘他们认为，如果你事先把这些零件送到太空待命，我们就会形成一个长期项目，国会也就不太容易放弃。’这一切原本计划用大约十年时间逐步落实——比如在2028年或2029年前完成。直到迈克·彭斯走上麦克风。

paragraphs.en: The American flag behind the vice president was large, almost comically so. Also behind him was an original space capsule from the Apollo days. Pence was exuding confidence that day in the spring of 2019. He proclaimed to the crowd at the Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville that “the first woman and the next man on the moon will both be American astronauts, launched by American rockets, from American soil.” He also announced out of nowhere that the schedule was cut in half. It would all be done in five years, by 2024. The end of what the administration hoped would be a second Trump term.
paragraphs.zh: 副总统身后飘扬的美国国旗又大又夸张，几乎有些喜感。其后还摆放着一枚阿波罗时代的原型舱。2019年春天的那一天，彭斯满脸自信。他在亨茨维尔的马歇尔航天中心对着人群宣布：‘在月球上的第一位女性和下一位男性都将是美国宇航员，由美国火箭从美国本土发射。’他还突然宣布，计划进度被砍半。全部任务将在五年内完成，也就是到2024年。这样一来，美国政府希望把所有成果都压在特朗普的第二任期末尾。

paragraphs.en: People in the room—and around the space agency—were stunned. As a former top NASA official recalls, “Everyone knew from the moment Pence said that, that it was pretty much bullshit.”
paragraphs.zh: 在场的人以及航天局周边的许多人都震惊了。正如一位前NASA高级官员回忆说：‘彭斯一开口，大家都知道，这基本上就是一派胡言。’

paragraphs.en: Reporters quizzed NASA’s leaders on the new deadline. “I do not like being—I will not lie,” a second former NASA official tells me. “So I said, ‘I think it’s a great goal, but like, this is going to be hard.’ And Jim [Bridenstine] told me later, he goes, ‘The one thing you need to start learning to say is: We will be on the moon in 2024.’”
paragraphs.zh: 记者们就这个新的最后期限问了NASA领导层。 “我不喜欢被问这个——我不会撒谎，”一位前NASA官员对我说，“所以我就说：‘我觉得这是个很好的目标，但这件事会很难。’后来，吉姆·布里登斯坦告诉我说：‘你现在要开始学着这样说了：我们将在2024年登月。’

paragraphs.en: It was a ridiculous commitment. Much of the gear to get back to the moon was still on the literal drawing board: NASA hadn’t even handed out contracts for the lunar landers yet. The space agency’s bureaucracy had a well-earned reputation for taking the maximum number of people and the maximum amount of time to get things done. “You know, 20 layers of boards and councils to get to a decision, or 480 people on a call. Do we really need that?” wonders a third former senior official.
paragraphs.zh: 这是一份荒唐的承诺。返回月球所需的大部分装备还只是停留在字面意义上的设计图纸上：NASA甚至还没有给月面着陆器发放合同。这家航天机构以耗费尽可能多的人力和时间来办事而闻名，早有名声。 “你看，光是为了做一个决策就要经过二十层委员会和理事会，或者一通电话有四百八十个人参与。我们真的需要这样吗？”一位第三位前高层官员这样发问。

paragraphs.en: The space program, once the avatar of American success, was becoming emblematic of American drift. Contractors complained about NASA’s endless requirements and safety checks; NASA officials complained about contractors’ empty factory floors and bankers’ hours; they both complained about the White House’s budget office micromanaging their launch dates; and absolutely everyone complained about the Senate force-feeding them tech they didn’t want.
paragraphs.zh: 这个曾经是美国成功代表的太空计划，正在变成美国举步维艰的象征。承包商抱怨NASA无休止的要求和安全检查；NASA官员抱怨承包商的空荡荡厂房和会计式的工时表；双方都抱怨白宫预算办公室对发射日期的微观干预；几乎每个人都在抱怨参议院强行给他们塞入他们不需要的技术。

paragraphs.en: Still, there were folks at NASA who were glad to have the 2024 deadline. After years of being lost in space, it was at least some sort of direction. Eight missions were sketched out for the Artemis program over the next decade. The pieces were already in place for the first two, an unmanned test of SLS and Orion that would orbit the moon and a manned lunar flyby. By the end of the first Trump administration, one last major component remained for Artemis III, the big one, the return of astronauts to the lunar surface. NASA had to award a contract to a company to design, build, and test the lander to bring astronauts down to the moon.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，NASA内部仍有人很高兴有了2024年的最后期限。经过多年在“失速”状态里漂泊，至少有了一个方向。阿耳忒弥斯计划在未来十年内拟定了八次任务。第一、二次任务的框架已经基本到位：一次SLS和猎户座飞船的无人测试任务，将绕月飞行，以及一次载人月球近掠。到特朗普首个任期结束时，阿耳忒弥斯三号还剩最后一个核心环节——也是最关键的一步——把宇航员送回月球表面。NASA必须给一家企业授予合同，让其设计、建造并测试着陆器，以将宇航员送上月球。

paragraphs.en: Jeff Bezos’ Blue Origin tried positioning itself as a major contender. But, as journalist Christian Davenport recounts in his book Rocket Dreams, Pence’s top aide told a Blue Origin exec he had a “Washington Post problem”—as in, the paper dared to criticize his boss’s big space speech. Joe Biden’s win in 2020 made that problem go away temporarily. But there was another issue: The bid Blue Origin submitted for the lander cost twice as much as Musk’s. (NASA and Blue Origin declined to comment on this story; SpaceX did not respond.)
paragraphs.zh: 杰夫·贝索斯的蓝色起源曾试图将自己定位为主要竞争者。但正如记者克里斯蒂安·达文波特在《Rocket Dreams》一书中所回忆的那样，彭斯的首席助手曾告诉一位蓝色起源高管，他有一个“华盛顿邮报问题”——也就是这家报纸敢于批评他的老板那场重要的太空演讲。2020 年乔·拜登的胜利让这个问题暂时消失了。但还有另一个问题：蓝色起源提交的着陆器方案，报价是马斯克方案的两倍。（NASA 和蓝色起源拒绝就此事发表评论；SpaceX 未作回应。）

paragraphs.en: SpaceX’s bid was in a class of its own. Its proposed lander was actually the second stage of its giganto, still-in-development rocket. The lander’s interior volume would be 150 times that of the Apollo lunar module, according to one SpaceX veteran, and more than that of the International Space Station. It was ludicrously big. “Like rowing across the Atlantic Ocean in a row boat and then taking a luxury cruise liner into the harbor,” is how a second SpaceX veteran put it. A “planet-colonizer,” is what Musk called it—his ride to his preferred destination, Mars. That’s why his bid was so much lower than Blue Origin’s; he wanted to build it anyway.
paragraphs.zh: SpaceX 的投标属于另一种级别。它提议的着陆器实际上是其“giganto”型号（仍在研发中的巨型火箭）第二级的一部分。根据一位 SpaceX 老兵的说法，该着陆器的内部体积是阿波罗登月舱的 150 倍，还大于国际空间站。这实在是夸张地巨大。另一位 SpaceX 老兵将其比作“在大西洋上划着小船划到对岸，再开豪华邮轮进港”。马斯克把它称为“行星殖民者”——他前往心仪目的地火星的航具。这也是为什么他的投标价格远低于蓝色起源；因为他本来就想把它建出来。

paragraphs.en: In between agency administrators, in 2021, NASA’s leaders gave Musk $3 billion for the lander. In some ways, they didn’t have a choice. The agency’s budget was impossibly tight. And if his design worked, it would supercharge human spaceflight for decades to come. But competitors grumbled; Blue Origin sued. The criticism grew louder when, years later, one of the NASA officials behind the decision took a job with Musk.
paragraphs.zh: 在机构管理层更迭期间，NASA 的领导层在 2021 年向马斯克拨款 30 亿美元用于该着陆器。从某种意义上说，他们并非真正有选择。该机构的预算紧张到了难以想象的地步。并且如果他的设计可行，将在未来几十年大幅提速载人航天。然而竞争对手对此表示不满，蓝色起源提起诉讼。当时这场争议又在几年后更响亮了，因为参与决策的某位 NASA 官员后来接受了马斯克的任职。

paragraphs.en: By then, Musk’s reputation for overpromising had been cemented. It was clear the space agency hadn’t fully understood the risks involved in Musk’s bid. That information gap would set the US lunar program back years.
paragraphs.zh: 到那时，马斯克“许诺过度”的声誉已经固化。很明显，NASA 当时并未充分理解其投标中存在的风险。这一信息缺口使美国月球计划倒退了多年。

paragraphs.en: You might ask yourself at this point why we should care about getting people on the moon or who gets there first. Truth be told, NASA and its contractors have had a hard time producing a consistent answer themselves.
paragraphs.zh: 你可能会在这个时候问自己，为什么我们要关心把人送上月球，或者谁先到达月球到底有什么意义。说实话，即便是NASA及其承包商自己，也很难给出一个始终一致的答案。

paragraphs.en: Some will tell you there’s a whole lunar economy waiting to be unleashed. Some will say that cislunar orbit is a great place to spy on (and even attack) your rival’s satellites. Some will tell you that exploration is a goal in and of itself. Some will ask you if you’ve seen one (1) science fiction movie in your life. Because then you’ve had the feeling, however fleeting, that absolutely nothing could be cooler than setting foot on an alien moon.
paragraphs.zh: 有人会告诉你，那里有一整套即将被释放的月球经济。有人会说，月地轨道是监视（甚至攻击）对手卫星的绝佳位置。有人会说探索本身就是一项目标。有人会反问你一生中是否看过哪怕一部（1）科幻电影。因为在那样的一瞬间你也会有这种感觉：登上异星卫星的月球，似乎没有什么比这更酷的了。

paragraphs.en: The mix of rationales helps explain why the Artemis program looks the way it does, a mismatched collection of leftovers and imports from the future, a blend of realpolitik and revolutionary. It’s a forced marriage between government contractor graybeards and billionaires with stars in their eyes. On the one hand, you’ve got a rocket and a capsule that can feel like unwieldy remakes of the Apollo program; on the other, an interplanetary “colonizer” and a freakin’ lunar space station. The collection is meant to support what an extremely understated former NASA official calls a “portfolio” approach to mission success. If Artemis hits any one of these many goals (jazzy lunar businesses, the next GPS-scale invention), the logic goes, the whole thing will have been worth it.
paragraphs.zh: 这些不同的理由拼在一起，帮助解释了Artemis计划为何会变成如今这副模样：一个由零散遗留技术和未来式拼件拼接而成的组合体，既有现实政治的考量，也有革命性的愿景。它更像一场“联姻”，把政府承包商的老资格老将和怀揣星辰梦想的亿万富翁硬拧在一起。一方面，你会看到的像猎猎而来的重制版阿波罗火箭与太空舱；另一方面，又有一艘“行星际殖民者”以及一座荒诞的月球空间站。这个组合的目标，是让一位极为低调的前NASA官员所说的“多元化任务组合”真正奏效。只要Artemis达到其中任何一个目标（比如诱人的月球商业模式，或下一次“GPS级别”的重大发明），逻辑上就会认为这整套计划都是值得的。

paragraphs.en: Maybe this makes sense to you, maybe it doesn’t. But here’s what everyone agrees on: The US and China are in a multifront contest for global influence. Beijing clearly has the momentum; now imagine its velocity after a moon landing. “If you’re trying to choose, who do I want to partner with? Whose team do I want to be on?” asks the American Enterprise Institute’s Todd Harrison, one of Washington’s sharper analysts of defense and space policy. “Do you want to be on the team that’s a bunch of has-beens? Or do you want to be on the team that is rapidly developing and has leapfrogged ahead of the United States?”
paragraphs.zh: 也许你会觉得这有道理，也许不会。但有一点大家都同意：美国和中国正在一场多战线的全球影响力竞争中交锋。北京显然已经拿到了势头；再想象一下它在实现月球登陆后所带来的速度。美国企业研究所（American Enterprise Institute）的托德·哈里森——华盛顿最敏锐的国防与太空政策分析师之一——问道：“如果你要选边站队，你会想和谁合作？你想加入哪支队伍？”他说：“你想加入一群已经不再前沿的‘往昔明星’吗？还是加入一支正快速发展、并已在关键领域超越美国的队伍？”

paragraphs.en: In September, Bridenstine testified before Cruz’s Senate committee, warning that the US plan was in real danger. He focused his remarks on Starship, the massive SpaceX craft that’s supposed to bring astronauts to the lunar surface. It’s “important for the country, and it’s transformational,” Bridenstine said. If and when it starts to work, that is. In the short term, it makes Artemis way more complicated. Starship is behind schedule, with a number of rather public explosions. But even if it were working perfectly, Starship offers a profoundly complicated ride to the moon. NASA officials didn’t realize how complicated until after they gave SpaceX billions to build their lander.
paragraphs.zh: 9月，布里登斯坦在克鲁兹的参议院委员会前作证，警告说美国的方案正处于真正危险之中。他的发言重点放在星舰上，这艘庞大的猎鹰航天器本应把宇航员送到月球表面。这种飞船“对国家非常重要，而且具有变革性”，布里登斯坦说——前提是它在将来真的能投入使用。短期来看，它让阿尔忒弥斯计划更为复杂。星舰进度滞后，还发生了不少公开的爆炸事故。但即使它运行完美，星舰前往月球的过程也异常复杂。NASA官员直到给SpaceX拨款数十亿美元用于建造登月器后，才意识到复杂程度之高。

paragraphs.en: A fully loaded, moon-bound Starship expends almost all of its fuel to get to low Earth orbit. So that Starship needs to refill its tank before it heads to the moon, requiring the launch of more and more refueling Starships.
paragraphs.zh: 一艘满载、飞向月球的星舰几乎要消耗掉全部推进剂才能到达近地轨道。因此，星舰在前往月球前必须先补充燃料，这就需要发射越来越多的加油星舰。

paragraphs.en: Starship runs on liquid oxygen and methane. They have to be kept ultracool, at –161 degrees Celsius or below. But direct exposure to the sun heats Starship up, evaporating some of that fuel. That could mean even more refueling missions to make up the difference. If things go really badly, the fuel might evaporate more quickly than SpaceX can get refueling rockets into space. “That whole in-space refueling thing has never been tested,” Bridenstine added. “We’re talking about cryogenic liquid oxygen, cryogenic liquid methane being transferred in space—never been done before, and we’re going to do it dozens of times.”
paragraphs.zh: 星舰使用液氧和甲烷作为推进剂。它们必须保持超低温，-161摄氏度及以下。但星舰暴露在阳光下会升温，导致一部分燃料蒸发。这可能意味着需要更多的补油任务来弥补损失。如果情况很糟，燃料可能蒸发得比SpaceX把补给火箭送入太空的速度还快。布里登斯тин补充说，“整个在轨加注的做法从未经过验证。我们现在要做的是在太空中转移液态氧和液态甲烷——这属于低温推进剂的空间转移，之前从未做过，而且我们要把这件事做几十次。”

paragraphs.en: Engineers at NASA’s Johnson Space Center recently calculated that one lunar mission could require more than 40 Starship launches. Refueling tests that were originally supposed to begin in 2023 are now “targeted to take place in 2026,” SpaceX says. As a reminder, China’s lunar mission will require two rockets.
paragraphs.zh: 美国宇航局约翰逊航天中心的工程师最近测算，单次月球任务可能需要超过40次星舰发射。SpaceX称，原本计划于2023年开始的加油试验现在“预计在2026年进行”。再提醒一句，中国的月球任务只需要两枚火箭。

paragraphs.en: This could’ve been a moment when the US changed course and declared that it doesn’t matter who repeats the Apollo landing first. Sean Duffy—the former reality star and secretary of transportation serving as acting NASA chief—took the opposite approach. He doubled down on beating China, and he blamed Starship for imperiling the lunar race. In October, Duffy said he’d formally ask other companies if they could build a lander faster than Musk. “We’re not going to wait for one company,” Duffy told CNBC. “We’re going to push this forward and win the second space race against the Chinese. Get back to the moon, set up a camp, a base.” He also gave SpaceX 10 days to submit a proposal for speeding up its lander effort.
paragraphs.zh: 这本可能是美国有机会改变方向，并宣布谁先实现阿波罗式登月并不重要的时刻。前《真实人生》明星、兼任代理NASA局长的交通部长肖恩·达菲（Sean Duffy）却采取了相反路线。他继续加码要击败中国，并把太空飞船Starship归咎于让月球竞赛陷入险境。10月，达菲说他将正式询问其他公司是否能比马斯克建造更快的着陆器。“我们不会只等一家公司的交付，”达菲对CNBC说，“我们会推进这项工作，并在第二次对阵中国的太空竞赛中赢得胜利。重返月球，建立一个营地，一个基地。”他还给了SpaceX 10天时间提交加快其着陆器计划的提案。

paragraphs.en: Musk responded with his usual maturity. He called the NASA chief “Sean Dummy” in a post on X. In another, Musk tweeted a meme at Duffy, saying, “Why are you gae?”
paragraphs.zh: 马斯克用他一贯的“成熟”方式回应了这番话。他在X上把NASA局长叫作“Sean Dummy”。在另一条帖子里，马斯克向达菲发了一个梗图，配文是：“Why are you gae?”

paragraphs.en: SpaceX wasn’t used to this kind of treatment. (“Here’s the way NASA works with SpaceX: They’ve learned to say, ‘You just keep blowing things up until you’ve got something that works a few times in a row. Then we’ll swoop in,’” one of the former NASA officials tells me.) But it was clear SpaceX was listening. A week after Musk’s mean tweets, the company said it was working on “a simplified mission architecture and concept of operations.”
paragraphs.zh: SpaceX过去并不习惯这样的对待方式。前NASA官员告诉我说：“NASA与SpaceX的合作方式是这样的：他们已经学会了说，‘你就继续把东西炸掉，直到你有几次连着成功。到那时，我们再进场。”）但很明显，SpaceX是在听的。马斯克那两条尖刻推文后一周，公司表示自己正在研究“更简化的任务架构和运行概念”。

paragraphs.en: Bezos’ company reacted quickly, too. During the Biden years, NASA had also given Blue Origin a contract for lunar landers. Blue Moon Mark 1 was a robotic model, meant for cargo missions. Blue Moon Mark 2 was bigger (though nowhere near Starship’s size) and meant to eventually carry astronauts. Team Bezos reportedly pitched Frankensteining them together into a sort of Mark 1.5, creating a human-ready version of Mark 1 with elements gleaned from Mark 2. Then the company announced a bigger version of its rocket that could carry more weight to lunar orbit.
paragraphs.zh: 贝索斯的公司也反应很快。在拜登任内，NASA也曾给蓝色起源（Blue Origin）下达了月球着陆器合同。Blue Moon Mark 1是一个机器人版本，用于货运任务。Blue Moon Mark 2更大（虽然远不及Starship的体积），并且将来可载人。贝索斯团队据称建议将两者“拼接”成一种Mark 1.5，形成一个可载人的Mark 1版本，吸收Mark 2的部分要素。随后，该公司宣布了其火箭的更大版本，可以运载更多质量到月球轨道。

paragraphs.en: A bunch of other aerospace companies are looking to join the race, as NASA evaluates SpaceX’s and Blue Origin’s accelerated proposals. Isaacman hasn’t said definitively whether he’ll continue Duffy’s push for a new lander contract. Actually building the thing would still take years.
paragraphs.zh: 随着NASA评估SpaceX和Blue Origin的加速方案，更多航天公司都在寻求加入这场竞赛。艾萨克曼尚未明确表示他是否会继续推动杜菲争取新的登月器合同。实际上，建造这种载具仍然需要数年时间。

paragraphs.en: Maybe a whole new lander will be able to beat China. The word is that it won’t—that SpaceX or Blue Origin present the only realistic, if unlikely, shots. Even if a new lander does somehow work, there’s fear in and around NASA that such a dash will sap the political will for long-term moon missions with potentially bigger payoffs. And this will be another case of planting flags in the lunar dust.
paragraphs.zh: 也许一款全新的登月器能够打败中国。传言是它不会——真正有现实希望、虽然可能性不高的只有SpaceX或Blue Origin。即便某个新登月器真的能成功，人们在NASA内外也担心，这样仓促的冲刺会消耗支持长期月球任务的政治意愿，而这些任务可能带来更大的回报。这很可能再次变成在月面尘土上插旗而已的一次尝试。

paragraphs.en: It certainly won’t paper over Artemis’ many uncertainties. By some estimates, each launch of the SLS rocket could cost as much as $4 billion, and then there are all the accompanying Starship launches. The rationale for the Gateway space station continues to get harder to parse. One space policy official tried to justify it to me by saying Gateway could be flown out to Mars if the moon thing doesn’t work out.
paragraphs.zh: 这当然无法掩盖“阿耳忒弥斯”计划的诸多不确定性。据某些估计，SLS火箭每次发射的成本高达40亿美元，此外还有配套的Starship发射任务。关于“门户号”空间站的论证，仍然越来越难以理解。曾有一位航天政策官员向我解释说，若月球任务不成，门户站可以转飞到火星。

paragraphs.en: And while NASA rushes to figure all of this out, the space agency is in turmoil.
paragraphs.zh: 而在NASA忙于把这些问题理顺之际，这个航天机构却陷入了混乱。

paragraphs.en: In November, with drama mounting around Artemis, Isaacman was suddenly renominated for the space job.
paragraphs.zh: 11月，随着阿耳忒弥斯计划周边戏剧性局势不断升级，艾萨克曼突然又被提名担任负责航天工作的职位。

paragraphs.en: No explanation was provided. Isaacman’s return was almost universally heralded in the space community; this is a guy famous for commanding the first all-civilian spaceflight, not for starring in The Real World: Boston. But it’s not clear how much of NASA will be left for him given the nearly 4,000 employees who accepted the Trump administration’s buyouts. Many top performers are gone. Hundreds more employees and contractors have been laid off. Project 2025 guru Russ Vought, who serves as the head of the White House’s Office of Management and Budget, has very specific ideas about space. His initial budget proposal to Congress slashed NASA’s funding by a quarter. One Trump administration official compared him to a vicious Civil War general: “He’s General Sherman on his march to the sea, burning down the government as he goes.”
paragraphs.zh: 没有给出任何解释。艾萨克曼的回归几乎在航天界获得普遍欢迎；他因指挥了首次全民间人员太空飞行而闻名，而不是因为出演过《真实世界：波士顿》。但在特朗普政府强行提出遣散补偿并被近4,000名员工接受之后，NASA还能为他留下多少空间，尚不清楚。许多顶尖员工已经离开。又有数百名员工和承包商被裁员。项目2025的专家鲁斯·沃思——他担任白宫管理与预算办公室主任——对太空有非常明确的看法。他提交给国会的初始预算提案削减了NASA四分之一的经费。一位特朗普政府官员把他比作一位凶狠的内战将领：“他像席夫军团的谢尔曼将军一样，行军到海边时一路放火把政府烧掉。”

paragraphs.en: Cruz swooped in to insert into a budget bill a combined $7 billion for SLS, Orion, and Gateway. The logic, at least for Orion and SLS, is that they’re already built, proven, and rated for human flight. But if you want a microcosm of the political psychosis gripping Washington, you could do worse. The Senate is foisting rockets that neither Duffy nor Isaacman wants long-term, all while planning to cut the agency to the bone. Is NASA a bloated bureaucracy? For sure. Is carpet-bombing it the best option? Unlikely.
paragraphs.zh: 克鲁兹插手在一项预算法案中，为SLS、猎户座飞船和“门户”计划合并拨款70亿美元。至少就猎户座和SLS而言，其逻辑是：它们已经建成、经验证明可靠，并且已经通过了载人飞行资格认证。但如果你想看一则映照华盛顿政治癫狂的缩影，恐怕也不会做得更差。参议院正在强推达菲和艾萨克曼都不想长期坚持的火箭计划，同时又计划把该机构“削到骨头”。NASA是个臃肿的官僚机构吗？毫无疑问。把它当成靶子大范围轰炸是最佳选择吗？并不大可能。

paragraphs.en: But it is of a piece. Under Trump, the US has declared China a geopolitical competitor—and tried to sabotage what competitive edges remain. Meanwhile, China is presenting itself as a logical inheritor of the Apollo program. Those early astronauts wanted us to think of Earth as our one, shared home. Today, that kind of language is considered too “woke” for Washington; the US’s space billionaires talk more about escaping this hellhole. It’s China that’s touting technologies to make Earth sustainable and positioning itself as the world’s stable trading partner. It’s luring scientists and engineers that the US is actively turning away with its immigration policies—a darkly ironic turn, given how much the original space race was run by immigrants.
paragraphs.zh: 但这正是同一出戏。特朗普执政时期，美国已将中国定性为地缘竞争对手，并试图破坏中国仍然保有的各项竞争优势。与此同时，中国正在把自己塑造成阿波罗计划的合逻辑继承者。早期的宇航员曾希望我们把地球视作共同的家园。如今，这样的说法在华盛顿被认为过于“觉醒”；美国的航天亿万富翁更常谈及逃离这个地狱般的地方。恰恰是中国在宣传使地球可持续发展的技术，并把自己定位为世界稳定的贸易伙伴。它正在吸引科学家和工程师，而美国却因移民政策而主动把他们逐步推开，这是一种深具讽刺意味的转折，尤其考虑到最初的太空竞赛很大程度上由移民主导。

paragraphs.en: It’ll take a shock to turn that around. But there are some who think that maybe it’d be OK for Beijing to have its Sputnik (or Apollo 11) moment. Maybe America needs to be surprised and shamed before it’ll get its act together. “There are definitely people in the space industry,” says one industry executive, “that see the Chinese landing on the moon before us as probably a net positive.” Maybe then Musk would really focus on building rockets, instead of spending so much of his time being a racist agitator online. Maybe Bezos’ rocket company will become a more serious competitor. Maybe then the United States will care about slipping to second-rate.
paragraphs.zh: 要扭转这一局面需要一次冲击。却有人认为，让北京拥有自己的一次“斯普特尼克式时刻”（或者阿波罗11号式突破）也许并非坏事。也许美国只有在被惊到并蒙羞后才会把事情做好。某位航天业高管说：“太空行业里确实有人认为，中国在我们之前登月，可能反而是件净利好。”这样一来，也许马斯克就会真正把精力放在造火箭上，而不是把大量时间花在网上当种族主义煽动者。也许贝索斯的火箭公司会成为更严肃的竞争者。也许那时美国才会在让自己滑向二流的问题上真正警醒。
